1: Hiram king of Tyre sends cedar timber, stonemasons, and carpenters to build David’s palace. As a theme, the recognition of David’s kingship by the nations anticipates the recognition of Christ as king. The Magi came from the east to honor the newborn king (Matthew 2:2); Pilate wrote ‘King of the Jews’ over the cross (John 19:19). Even those outside the covenant recognize the divinely appointed king — the Davidic pattern fulfilled in the one whom even pagan rulers are compelled to acknowledge.
2: ‘David perceived that the LORD had established him as king over Israel and that his kingdom was highly exalted for the sake of his people Israel.’ The Davidic king understands his exaltation as covenantal and missional — for the people, not for himself. As a revelation of God: the God who exalts kings does so for the benefit of his people. The NT applies this to Christ: his exaltation to God’s right hand is ‘for us’ — he intercedes (Romans 8:34), sends the Spirit (Acts 2:33), and will bring his people to be where he is (John 14:3).
3: David took more wives and fathered more sons and daughters in Jerusalem. The dynasty expands at its Jerusalem center. As a theme, the multiplication of David’s house points toward the ultimate heir through whom the dynasty will be fulfilled. The NT genealogies trace specific lines (Matthew through Solomon; Luke through Nathan) to show how the many sons of David converge on one Son of David. The expansion recorded here is the necessary background for the genealogical narrowing that eventually reaches Christ.
4: Among David’s sons: Shammua, Shobab, Nathan, Solomon. Nathan (third in the list) is the ancestor of Mary’s lineage of Christ in Luke 3:31 — the non-royal, non-Solomonic line through which the Messiah actually came into the world. The naming of Nathan in this list establishes the genealogical line even as the Chronicler focuses on Solomon (the temple-builder). The two Messianic genealogies diverge here at David’s Jerusalem sons: Matthew traces Joseph’s legal line through Solomon; Luke traces Mary’s biological line through Nathan.
5: Ibhar, Elishua, Elpelet — three more of David’s sons, whose names include theophoric elements (ʾēl, God). The expansion of the Davidic house with God-bearing names reflects the theological identity of the royal line: this is the family through whom God will accomplish his purposes. The theme: David’s household is not merely a political dynasty but the vehicle of the covenant promise, expanding outward toward the one in whom all these names find their referent — ‘God with us’ (Matthew 1:23).
6: Nogah, Nepheg, Japhia — three more sons. The Davidic genealogy continues its expansion. Each name is a branch that the NT genealogists navigate to trace the Messianic line. As a theme: even the branches that are not the direct Messianic line are part of the Davidic family that establishes Christ’s dynastic credentials. ‘He will be great and will be called the Son of the Most High. And the Lord God will give to him the throne of his father David’ (Luke 1:32).
7: Elishama, Beeliada, Eliphelet. As a theme, these sons complete the roster of David’s Jerusalem-born children. The Chronicler notes Beeliada (retaining the Baal-theophoric form where 2 Samuel 5:16 has Eliada) — reflecting historical accuracy even in uncomfortable details. The revelation of God: divine history does not sanitize its instruments; the Davidic house includes sons with names honoring other gods, yet the covenant proceeds. Christ was ‘made in human likeness’ (Philippians 2:7) — he entered the fullness of human genealogical complexity.
8: The Philistines come against David when they hear he is anointed king over all Israel. As a type, the hostile response of the powers to the anointed king mirrors the response of the spiritual powers to Christ’s anointing. At Christ’s baptism (‘This is my beloved Son’, Matthew 3:17), the wilderness temptation follows immediately. Revelation 12:4-5 depicts the dragon waiting to devour the royal child. The pattern: the anointed one provokes the opposition of the powers by his very appointment.
9: The Philistines spread out in the Valley of Rephaim (the valley of the ancient giants, southwest of Jerusalem). As a shadow, the enemies gathering in the valley of the giants at the approach of the anointed king images the gathering of the powers against Christ. Acts 4:26-27 quotes Psalm 2 (‘the kings of the earth took their stand … against the LORD and against his anointed’) and applies it to the gathering against Jesus: ‘for truly in this city there were gathered together against your holy servant Jesus … Herod and Pontius Pilate.’
10: ‘David inquired of God.’ This is the type: the king who only acts after seeking the Father. John 5:19 is the NT fulfillment in Christ: ‘The Son can do nothing of his own accord, but only what he sees the Father doing.’ John 5:30: ‘I can do nothing on my own.’ David’s inquiry before battle is the shadow of Christ’s perfect dependence — the Davidic pattern made explicit in Christ who fulfills it perfectly. YHWH answers: ‘Go up, for I will give them into your hand.’
11: Baal-perazim — ‘the Lord of the breakthroughs.’ David declares: ‘God has broken through my enemies by my hand, like a breakthrough of waters.’ As a type, this breakthrough-victory anticipates Christ’s resurrection — the ultimate breaking through. Death could not hold him (Acts 2:24); the gates of Hades could not prevail (Matthew 16:18). The name Baal-perazim is reclaimed: not Baal but YHWH is the one who breaks through. Christ is the resurrection and the life who breaks through the ultimate barrier.
12: The Philistines abandoned their gods on the battlefield; David burned them. As a shadow, this anticipates Christ’s victory over the powers: ‘he disarmed the rulers and authorities and put them to open shame, by triumphing over them in him’ (Colossians 2:15). The gods of the enemy are revealed as impotent — they were left behind by their own worshippers. Christ’s cross exposes and defeats the principalities; the burning of the Philistine gods is the small-scale image of the definitive victory over all false gods.
13: A second Philistine incursion — the defeated enemy returns. As a theme, the persistence of opposition to the anointed king mirrors the persistence of opposition to Christ. After every apparent victory, the enemy regrouped. Christ himself warns of this: ‘the thief comes only to steal and kill and destroy’ (John 10:10); the spirits return (Luke 11:24-26). The proper response is always renewed inquiry of God — not self-reliance based on prior success.
14: ‘Do not go up after them; go around.’ God gives David a different tactical instruction for the second battle — the same enemy requires fresh dependence, not cached strategy. As a revelation of God: YHWH is not a formula but a living guide whose specific direction must be sought each time. Christ teaches the same principle: the Spirit ‘will guide you into all the truth’ (John 16:13) — not a static set of rules but ongoing living guidance from the one who goes before.
15: ‘When you hear the sound of marching in the tops of the balsam trees, then go out to battle, for God has gone out before you.’ The divine warrior going before his people — YHWH leading the battle — is the OT military theology. As a type, this prefigures Christ as the pioneer and perfecter (Hebrews 12:2: archēgon, the one who goes first, leads the way). Revelation 19:11-14 depicts Christ going before his armies on a white horse. The sound in the treetops is the advance movement of the one who always goes before.
16: ‘David did as God commanded, and they struck down the Philistine army from Gibeon to Gezer.’ Obedience to specific divine word produces comprehensive victory. The scope (Gibeon to Gezer, a broad sweep clearing the western approaches) reflects the proportionality of the victory to the divine command followed. As a revelation of God: YHWH’s specific commands, when fully followed, produce comprehensive results. Christ fulfills this in himself: ‘I have come down from heaven not to do my own will but the will of him who sent me’ (John 6:38).
17: ‘The LORD brought the fear of him upon all nations.’ David’s fame through divine power echoes the covenant promise of Deuteronomy 28:10. As a revelation of God, this outcome points toward the Messianic prophecies of universal recognition: ‘at the name of Jesus every knee should bow, in heaven and on earth and under the earth, and every tongue confess that Jesus Christ is Lord, to the glory of God the Father’ (Philippians 2:10-11). The fear that preceded the nations before the Davidic warrior becomes the ultimate submission before the Messianic king.
